title: Solid Polymer Electrolytes, 2. Preparation and Ionic Conductivity of Solid Polymer Electrolytes Based on Segmented Polysiloxane-Modified Polyurethane
abstract:
230
Solid Polymer Electrolytes, 2
Preparation and Ionic Conductivity of Solid Polymer
Electrolytes Based on Segmented Polysiloxane-
Modified Polyurethane
Ping-Lin Kuo*, Wuu-Jyh Liang, Chia-Liang Lin
Department of Chemical Engineering, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan, Taiwan 70101, ROC
Keywords: ionic conductivity; poly(ethylene glycol); polymer electrolytes; polysiloxanes; polyurethanes;
Introduction
Solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs) are used for recharge-
able batteries and electronic devices with the increased
request in performance.[1] Consequently, research in this
field has attracted a wide range of interest both for aca-
demic and industrial purposes. Due to the solvation
power and complexing ability of poly(ethylene oxide)
(PEO) to alkali metal ions, many studies[2–11] on SPEs
have dealt with the systems consisting of polyether alike
structures with lithium salts. The migration of ions is per-
ceived to take place in amorphous phase of PEO.[12]
Increasing temperatures are favorable for the occurrence
of amorphous phase resulting in higher conductivity, but
also reduce physical properties of the polymer matrix.
To prepare a solid polymer electrolyte for practical
application, the high conductivity and the good physical
properties such as chemical stability and mechanical
strength have to be considered in the mean time regarding
Full Paper: Segmented polyurethanes (PSEU) were
synthesized from polydimethylsiloxane diol (PSi) mixed
with poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) in different ratios as
soft segment, 4,49-diphenylmethane diisocyanate as hard
segment, and ethylene glycol as chain extender. FT-IR,
NMR, and thermal analysis were used to characterize the
structure and morphology of these copolymers. These
copolymers were then swollen in a LiClO4/PC liquid elec-
trolyte solution to obtain gel-like polymer electrolytes.
The Li+ ions are more effectively adsorbed in the micro-
phase of PEG, however, the existence of polysiloxane sig-
nificantly improves the property of solvent resistance.
Then, impedance spectroscopy was used to investigate the
conductivity of these polymers electrolytes as a function
of the content of LiClO4/PC liquid electrolyte. The ionic
conductivity of these systems reaches an order of
2.33610–3 S N cm–1 at 808C and 5.9610–4 S N cm–1 at
258C, respectively, where the films with the increase of
50 wt.-% immersed in 1 m LiClO4/PC are homogenous
and exhibit good mechanical properties. The correlation
between the structure and the ion-conducting behavior of
these copolymers was interpreted by the results of DSC in
the presence or absence of Li+.
Macromol. Chem. Phys. 2002, 203, No. 1

Arrhenius
plots of conductivity for PEU, PSEU30/70,
PSEU50/50, and PSEU70/30 polymer films with the increase
of 50 wt.-% immersed in 1 m LiClO4/PC.
Macromol. Chem. Phys. 2002, 203, 230–237
 Solid Polymer Electrolytes, 2
231
to its structure. Since polyether acts as polymer solvent
favoring the transportation of ions, polysiloxane (PSi) has
a high potential to be used as SPEs not only because of
the existence of ether moiety inside it but also because of
its chemical and thermal stability.[13]
In order to acquire the high conductivity and the good
physical properties, a series of cross-linked Si/PEG copo-
lymers were prepared and studied in our previous
work.[14] To obtain the SPEs with high mechanical
strength, in this study, a series of high molecular weight
of polyurethanes containing polysiloxane and PEG seg-
ments were synthesized by a reaction of diol terminated
by isocyanate and a reaction of chain-extension to obtain
the character like thermal plastic polyurethane (TPU).
The structures of the prepared copolymers were then
characterized by FT-IR, NMR and DSC. These results are
correlated and used to interpret the phenomena of con-
ductivity of lithium ion in the matrix of PSi/PEG copoly-
mers.
Experimental Part
Materials
Poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG, MW = 1000, Showa) and poly-
dimethylsiloxanediol (FM-4411, MW = 1000, Chiso) were
dehydrated by azeotropic distillation withtoluene. 4,49-
Diphenylmethane diisocyanate (MDI, Acros) was heated in
an oven at 658C and only the upper clear solution was
removed for the further synthesis. N,N-Dimethylacetamide
(DMAc, Tedia) and ethylene glycol (EG, Tedia) were dried
over calcium hydride and vacuum distilled prior to use. Pro-
pylene carbonate (PC, Acros), lithium perchlorate (Aldrich)
and all other chemicals were used as received.
Methods
Polymerization
All segmented polyurethanes PEU, PSEU30/70, PSEU50/50,
PSEU70/30, and PSU were synthesized by a two-step addi-
tion reaction according to Scheme 1 where FM-4411/PEG
mixed with different ratios, i.e., FM-4411/PEG = 0/100, 30/
70, 50/50, 70/30 or 100/0, respectively. To this mixture in a
500 ml three-necked flask, MDI (the equivalent ratio of OH/
NCO is equal to 0.5) and DMAc (300 ml) were introduced.
This flask was flushed with a slow stream of dry nitrogen
during the addition. The mixture was then heated and stirred
at 808C for 10 h. Then it was cooled to 608C and an equiva-
lent amount of EG was added for 3 h. After the solvent was
removed under reduced pressure, the viscous solution was
poured into water to precipitate the polymer. The product
was dried in a vacuum oven at 808C for 48 h.
Preparation of Solid Polymer Complexes
The desired lithium salt-doped polymer complex was pre-
pared by dissolving the PSEU50/50 in DMAc with proper
LiClO4/DMAc solution. The solution was then cast under
vacuum at 808C for 48 h to form 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 mmol
LiClO4/g PSEU50/50 polymer films. Upon removal of the
solvent, a separate salt phase was not observed.
Preparation of Gelled Polymer Electrolytes and Swelling
Study
Polymer films were prepared by casting a DMAc solution of
copolymer onto aluminum plates, followed by evaporating
the solution at 858C. The films obtained were dried comple-
tely under vacuum at 808C for 48 h and all the resulting
films were approximately 200 lm thick. These specimen
were
then
transferred
into
an argon-filled
glove box
(Vacuum Atmosphere, USA) for cell assembly. To obtain
composite electrolytes, the polymer films were dipped in a
1 m LiClO4/PC solution for different periods of time to
obtain various contents of LiClO4/PC. The swollen percent-
age of polymer film was defined as:

strength have to be considered in the mean time regarding

dimethylsiloxanediol (FM-4411, MW = 1000, Chiso) were
dehydrated by azeotropic distillation withtoluene. 4,49-
Diphenylmethane diisocyanate (MDI, Acros) was heated in
an oven at 658C and only the upper clear solution was
removed for the further synthesis. N,N-Dimethylacetamide
(DMAc, Tedia) and ethylene glycol (EG, Tedia) were dried
over calcium hydride and vacuum distilled prior to use. Pro-
pylene carbonate (PC, Acros), lithium perchlorate (Aldrich)
and all other chemicals were used as received.
All segmented polyurethanes PEU, PSEU30/70, PSEU50/50,
PSEU70/30, and PSU were synthesized by a two-step addi-
tion reaction according to Scheme 1 where FM-4411/PEG
mixed with different ratios, i.e., FM-4411/PEG = 0/100, 30/
70, 50/50, 70/30 or 100/0, respectively. To this mixture in a
500 ml three-necked flask, MDI (the equivalent ratio of OH/
NCO is equal to 0.5) and DMAc (300 ml) were introduced.
This flask was flushed with a slow stream of dry nitrogen
during the addition. The mixture was then heated and stirred
at 808C for 10 h. Then it was cooled to 608C and an equiva-
lent amount of EG was added for 3 h. After the solvent was
removed under reduced pressure, the viscous solution was
poured into water to precipitate the polymer. The product
was dried in a vacuum oven at 808C for 48 h.

pared by dissolving the PSEU50/50 in DMAc with proper
LiClO4/DMAc solution. The solution was then cast under
vacuum at 808C for 48 h to form 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 mmol
LiClO4/g PSEU50/50 polymer films. Upon removal of the
solvent, a separate salt phase was not observed.

copolymer onto aluminum plates, followed by evaporating
the solution at 858C. The films obtained were dried comple-
tely under vacuum at 808C for 48 h and all the resulting
films were approximately 200 lm thick. These specimen

AC impedance measurements of the polymer electrolytes
were performed using a EG&G Model 5210 impedance ana-
lyzer coupled to EG&G Potentiostat/Galvanostat 273A
power supplier over a frequency range of 100 Hz–100 kHz
with an amplitude of 10 mV. All the specimen were sand-
wiched by two polished stainless steel (SS304) blocking
electrodes and the cells were sealed with O-ring for conduc-
tivity tests. The presenting conductivity values are obtained

where Rb is the intercept of the straight line and real axis in
the Nyquist plot, l and A represent the film thickness and sur-
face area of an electrode, respectively.
In this study, a series of polyurethanes (PEU, PSEU30/
70, PSEU50/50, PSEU70/30, and PSU) were prepared
from different ratios of hydroxy terminated polysiloxane
(PSi)/poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) to be 0/100, 30/70, 50/
50, 70/30, and 100/0, respectively. The mixture of PSi/
PEG was reacted with MDI in the solvent of DMAc with
the OH/NCO ratio to be 0.5, then the 1NCO terminated
PSi/PEG was further reacted with the chain extender EG
to obtain the high molecular weight PU which can not be
dissolved in THF for the measurement of molecular
weight by GPC. As pointed out in ref.[15] there exists the
inevitable side reaction between 1NH1CO and 1NCO
at the high ratio or high concentration of 1NCO. How-
ever, the obtained PU still can be dissolved in aprotic sol-
vents like DMF and DMAc usually used for PU, indicat-
ing that its crosslinking is still not too high. All of the
reprecipitated products are transparent elastomers with
FT-IR is a convenient method to trace the reaction of
isocyanate for polyurethane preparation. A FT-IR spec-
trum for the polydimethylsiloxane contained copolymer
represented by PSEU50/50 is shown in Figure 1. The
characteristic peak of isocyanate at 2275 cm–1 disappears,
indicating the complete conversion of isocyanate to poly-
urethane. The absorption peaks around 3320 cm–1 (urea
N1H stretching) and 1645 cm–1 (H-bonded urea C2O)
are assigned to be the urea linkage. The peak at 3331
cm–1 (urethane N1H stretching), 1700 cm–1 (H-bonded
urethane C2O), and 1080 cm–1 (C1O1C) stretch show
the formation of the urethane linkage. The peak at 1613
cm–1 (benzene ring stretching) evidences the existence of
aromatic ring from MDI. The peaks at 1260 cm–1 (sym.
CH3 bending), 1020 and 1100 cm–1 (Si1O1Si stretch-
ing), 803 cm–1 (CH3 rocking) are assigned to the polydi-
methylsiloxane in the copolymer, indicating the incor-
poration of polysiloxane into the polyurethane.
PSEU50/50 was also verified by 1H NMR as shown in
Figure 2. The peaks at d = 9.1 and 8.2 ppm are due to
urethane and urea protons, and those at d = 7.1, 7.4,
3.8 ppm are assigned to be aromatic protons and benzyl
protons on MDI. Methylene protons from EG and PEG
are also observed at d = 3.4, 4.2, 3.6 ppm and the protons
of methylene at two terminals of polydimethylsiloxane
are at d = 3.7, 3.6, 3.1, 1.5, and 0.6 ppm. The protons
associated with the methyl group attached to silicon show
a large peak at 0.1 ppm. These results from the NMR
clearly illustrate that polysiloxane, poly(ethylene glycol)
The FT-IR spectra for (a) PEG 1000, (b) hydroxy
terminated polysiloxane (FM-4411), and (c) PSEU50/50.
and MDI have been successfully incorporated to form the
copolymers of polyurethane (PSEU), and the fed and
obtained molar ratio with respect to PEG, PDMS and
MDI for all these copolymers are tabulated in Table 1.
Thermal properties of the copolymers (PEU, PSEU30/70,
PSEU50/50, PSEU70/30, and PSU) were investigated
using DSC and TGA. Displayed in Figure 3 are DSC
results for the copolymers. The glass transition tempera-
tures (Tg) of soft segment of PEG and polysiloxane can
be observed at –238C and –1078C, respectively. For the
copolymers of PEG/polysiloxane (PSEU30/70, PSEU50/
50, and PSEU70/30), the Tg’s of PEG can be observed
clearly, but those of polysiloxane are less obvious. The
slight inflection of DSC curve in the range of 60–1008C
can be assigned to be the Tg of hard segment of urethane
groups from MDI like the reported Tg for urethane.[16] The
less obvious inflection can be attributed to the relatively
small content of hard segment in PSi/PEG copolymers.
Except to the Tg of hard segment, PSEU70/30 shows
clearly two thermal transitions corresponding to Tg for the
domains in the copolymer, respectively, indicating that
the microphase of polydimethylsiloxane separated from
the PEG soft segment and hard segment phase.
The weight loss behavior of the copolymers were
investigated by TGA scanning from 50 to 8008C under
nitrogen as shown in Figure 4. The polydimethylsiloxane
contained PU show clearly a two-stage weight loss
behavior, which can not be observed for the homogenous
PEG contained PU (PEU). The temperature of initial 10%
weight loss (T0.1) of all copolymers occurs around 3858C,
The NMR spectrum for PSEU50/50 (in DMSO-d6).
The molar content (x, y, z) with respect to PEG,
PDMS and MDI for all these copolymers.
a) Theoretical value means the fed molar ratio of PDMS/PEG/
b) Actual value means the actual molar ratio of PDMS/PEG/MDI
in the obtained copolymers, determined by 1H NMR spectra.
P.-L. Kuo, W.-J. Liang, C.-L. Lin
indicating that all of the copolymers show excellent ther-
mal stability. The results from Figure 4 show that the
incorporation of polydimethylsiloxane does not influence
Figure 5 shows the DSC thermograms of PSEU50/50
LiClO4 concentrations. For the salt-free PSEU50/50, the
Tg of the soft segment appeared at –22.58C and increases
with the increasing LiClO4 concentration from 0 to 0.5
mmol/g, consistent with other investigations of TPUs
containing either PEO or PPO[17–21] as the soft segment.
This indicates that the solvation of the lithium cation
(Li+) by the PEO soft-segment partially interrupts the
local motion of the polymer segment through the forma-
tion of transient crosslinks, leading to an increase in the
Tg of soft segment. As the LiClO4 concentration is above
0.5 mmol/g, the Tg of the soft segment decreases with
increasing concentrations. This result is attributed to the
plasticizing effect generated by the formation of charge-
neutral ion pairs as increasing LiClO4 concentration.[22]
Figure 6 presents the DSC thermogram for the copoly-
mer PSEU50/50 immersed in 1 m LiClO4/PC, showing an
increase of 50 wt.-%. It is obvious that the Tg of the soft-
segment has decreased from –22.58C to –958C, indicat-
ing that the degree of solvation of Li+ by the oxygen of
decreased Tg is owing to the enhanced mobility of the
soft-segment by PC solvent, resulting in the increase the
ionic conductivity of polymer electrolytes.[23]
Swelling Study and A.C. Impedance Analysis
The prepared copolymers with approximately 200 lm
thickness were dipped into 1 m LiClO4/PC solutions and

lyte with (a) no LiClO4/PC, and (b) the increase of 50 wt.-%
the swelling percentage by weight was measured versus
various immersion times and the results were plotted in
Figure 7, and the total amount of LiClO4 and the PC con-
tent at various swelling percentages for gelled polymer
electrolytes are shown in Table 2. The swelling rate and
the saturated swelling percentage can be obtained from
the slope and the plateau of the curves, respectively. It is
clear that the swelling rate in the initial stage of these
copolymers is in the order PEU A PSEU30/70 A PSEU70/
30 > PSEU50/50. Also, the saturated swelling percentage
increases with the decreasing ratio of polysiloxane/PEG,
i.e., PEU L PSEU30/70 A PSEU50/50 A PSEU70/30, in-
dicating that ions are more effectively absorbed in the
microphase of soft PEG. PEU can be immersed only for
90 min, relative to PSEU30/70 to be of more than 160
min. This phenomenon demonstrates that the existence of
polysiloxane significantly improves the solvent resistance
Figure 8 shows the a.c. impedance spectra of the
PSEU50/50 attached on stainless steel cells as a function
of content of LiClO4/PC at 258C. The resistance (Rb) of
polymer electrolyte obtained from the intercept on the
real axis decreases with the increasing content of LiClO4/
PC in the polymer electrolyte. The increasing ionic con-
ductivity due to the enhancement of the ionic mobility
can be attributed to the increasing number of carrier
ions.[23] It is noted that the semi-circle observed at high

time for PEU, PSEU30/70, PSEU50/50, and PSEU70/30 poly-
The total amount of LiClO4 and the PC content at var-
ious swelling percentages for gelled polymer electrolytes.
Nyquist plot of the PSEU50/50 film attached on
stainless steel cells at 258C; 300 lm thick and 0.785 cm2 area
for the film. Impedance frequency from 100 kHz to 100 Hz with
the increase of (a) 25 wt.-%, and (b) 50 wt.-% immersed in 1 m
P.-L. Kuo, W.-J. Liang, C.-L. Lin
frequency disappears as the content of LiClO4/PC
increases to 50 wt.-% of the film immersed in 1 m
LiClO4/PC. This result suggests that only the resistive
component of polymer electrolyte can be considered, as
the content of LiClO4/PC is high. For the polymer elec-
trolytes with lower content of LiClO4/PC, the migration
of ions may occur through the free volume of polymer
matrix to act as a resistor. The immobile polymer chains
become polarized in the alternating field to act as a capa-
citor. The ionic migration and bulk polarization are physi-
cally in parallel, therefore, the semicircle at high fre-
quency can be observed. As the content of LiClO4/PC
increases, it is possible to construct a local effective path-
way in liquid phase for ionic conduction. Therefore, no
capacitance effect was occurred in the polymer electro-
lytes with higher content of LiClO4/PC.
Figure 9 shows the temperature dependence of ionic
conductivity with different LiClO4/PC contents for the
PSEU50/50-LiClO4/PC complexes. It exists a common
trend that the lower LiClO4/PC content results in a
greater slope than the higher LiClO4/PC content. This is
related to conducting pathway in the polymer electrolytes
as explained above. As the content of LiClO4/PC exceeds
about 50 wt.-%, the added LiClO4/PC can form a local
solvent channel for ionic conduction, which is the major
medium governing the ionic conductivity. In other words,
local pathways of LiClO4/PC can be constructed in
PSEU50/50-LiClO4/PC complexes whose contents of
LiClO4/PC is higher than 50 wt.-%.
The Arrhenius plots of ionic conductivities for PEU,
PSEU30/70, PSEU50/50, and PSEU70/30 polymer elec-
trolytes containing 50 wt.-% LiClO4/PC are shown in
Figure 10. For the PSEU30/70 polymer electrolyte, the
conductivity reaches an order of 2.33610–3
S N cm–1 at 808C, and 5.9610–4 S N cm–1 at 258C. The
ionic conductivities were found to increase with the
increasing content of PEG. The difference in conductivity
mainly arises from the content and property of soft seg-
ments rather than the hard segment domain of MDI phase
in all these copolymers. The higher PEG content attracts
the higher amount of Li+ and results in a higher conduc-
Segmented polyurethane copolymers have been success-
fully synthesized from polydimethylsiloxane diol, poly-
(ethylene glycol), MDI, and ethylene glycol. The struc-
ture and morphology of these copolymers were character-
ized by FT-IR, NMR, and DSC. It was observed that the
Tg of the soft-segment of these copolymers varies with
various LiClO4 concentrations, and is decreased remark-
ably by PC solvent. These copolymers also show good
thermal stability as indicated from T0.1 in TGA. The ionic
conductivity of these copolymer electrolytes is influenced
by the composition, the content of LiClO4/PC liquid elec-
trolyte, and temperature. The higher temperature and the
higher content of PEG and LiClO4/PC in these copolymer
electrolytes results in the higher ionic conductivity. The
relationship between conductivity and temperature for
these copolymer electrolytes follows Arrhenius law. It is
found that the film of PSEU30/70 immersed in 1 m
LiClO4/PC has an increase of 50 wt.-% and exhibits the
Ionic conductivity of the PSEU50/50 polymer elec-
trolyte as functions of liquid electrolyte content and tempera-
Arrhenius plots of conductivity for PEU, PSEU30/
70, PSEU50/50, and PSEU70/30 polymer films with the
increase of 50 wt.-% immersed in 1 m LiClO4/PC.
2.33610–3 S N cm–1 at 808C and shows good physical
properties. This indicates that PSEU polymer electrolytes
do have a high potential to possess satisfactory properties
used for practical applications in the advanced lithium
